masthead.title_html: Reason Your Way Through Food & Drink, Vol. 2
masthead.title_plain: Reason Your Way Through Food & Drink, Vol. 2
masthead.dek: A fresh round on what we eat and drink — true origins, beverages, and the records and science behind them. Every answer beats a reflex (the biggest producer isn't the country you'd name first; the food isn't from where its name says). Narrow it down, place your bet, then tap to check.
masthead.footer_html: Built fresh against the existing Food & Drink set. Verified to Britannica, FAO/FAOSTAT, OIV, the ICO, and food-history sources.

plan.note: Three branches (true origins, beverages, records/history/science), answers routing to countries, decades, counts, and foods, each with a reflex-defeater.

questions.cat: Food & Drink · native continent of a misnamed vegetable
questions.dimension: native continent of a misnamed vegetable
questions.q: The Jerusalem artichoke is neither an artichoke nor from Jerusalem. On which continent does this knobby vegetable actually grow wild as a native plant?
questions.detail: It is the tuber of a wild sunflower (Helianthus tuberosus) native to eastern North America, cultivated by Native Americans long before Europeans saw it.
questions.why: The name points to the Middle East, but 'Jerusalem' is a garbled version of the Italian word girasole, meaning sunflower; the plant is fully American.
questions.more: Champlain reported Native Americans growing it on Cape Cod in 1605, and it reached Europe by 1612.
questions.source: Britannica, 'Jerusalem artichoke' (https://www.britannica.com/plant/Jerusalem-artichoke)

questions.cat: Food & Drink · true country a famous dish was invented
questions.dimension: true country a famous dish was invented
questions.q: Hawaiian pizza, topped with ham and pineapple, was not invented in Hawaii. In which country did a restaurant cook first put it together?
questions.detail: Sam Panopoulos, a Greek-born cook, first made it in 1962 at his restaurant in Chatham, Ontario, naming it after the canned-pineapple brand he used.
questions.why: The name suggests the islands, but the islands had nothing to do with it; the dish is a Canadian creation.
questions.more: Panopoulos said it was 'an invention by accident,' inspired partly by sweet-and-savory Chinese-American food.
questions.source: The Canadian Encyclopedia, 'Hawaiian Pizza' (https://www.thecanadianencyclopedia.ca/en/article/hawaiian-pizza)

questions.cat: Food & Drink · European parent dish of an Indian curry
questions.dimension: European parent dish of an Indian curry
questions.q: The fiery Indian curry called vindaloo grew out of a marinated-meat dish brought by sailors from which European country?
questions.detail: Vindaloo descends from the Portuguese 'carne de vinha d'alhos' (meat in wine and garlic), brought to Goa in the early 1500s and reworked with palm vinegar and chilies.
questions.why: People assume it is a homegrown Indian recipe, but its very name comes from the Portuguese words for wine and garlic.
questions.more: The Portuguese also introduced the American chili peppers that later made the dish so hot.
questions.source: Wikipedia, 'Vindaloo' and 'Carne de vinha d'alhos'; Taste of Lisboa (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vindaloo)

questions.cat: Food & Drink · native continent of a misnamed fruit tree
questions.dimension: native continent of a misnamed fruit tree
questions.q: Tamarind's name means 'Indian date,' but the sour pod tree is actually native to which continent?
questions.detail: Tamarindus indica is native to tropical Africa; it was carried to India so long ago that Arab traders named it tamar hindi, 'Indian date.'
questions.why: Both the common name and the scientific name 'indica' point to India, but the tree's true home is African.
questions.more: From India the name spread to Persians and Arabs, locking in the mistaken Indian label.
questions.source: Oxford Plants 400, 'Tamarindus' (https://herbaria.plants.ox.ac.uk/bol/plants400/Profiles/st/Tamarindus)

questions.cat: Food & Drink · native country of a familiar nut tree
questions.dimension: native country of a familiar nut tree
questions.q: The cashew tree, prized in Indian cooking, was first found by Europeans in which country, its true native home?
questions.detail: The cashew (Anacardium occidentale) is native to northeastern Brazil; Portuguese traders carried it to East Africa and India in the 1500s.
questions.why: Cashews feel quintessentially Indian, yet India only grows them because Portuguese ships brought the Brazilian tree there.
questions.more: The English word 'cashew' comes through Portuguese from the Tupi word acaju.
questions.source: Britannica, 'Cashew' (https://www.britannica.com/plant/cashew)

questions.cat: Food & Drink · native country of a renamed fruit
questions.dimension: native country of a renamed fruit
questions.q: Before marketers renamed it the kiwifruit, this fuzzy green fruit was called the 'Chinese gooseberry' because it came from which country?
questions.detail: The fruit is native to China; New Zealand growers renamed it 'kiwifruit' in 1959 to dodge the Cold-War-unfriendly 'Chinese' label.
questions.why: The 'kiwi' name and New Zealand fame hide the fact that seeds were only carried out of China in 1904.
questions.more: China is again the world's top kiwifruit grower today, ahead of New Zealand.
questions.source: NZ History, 'Chinese gooseberry becomes kiwifruit' (https://nzhistory.govt.nz/chinese-gooseberry-becomes-kiwifruit)

questions.cat: Food & Drink · true native region of a meat bird
questions.dimension: true native region of a meat bird
questions.q: The Thanksgiving turkey shares a name with a country, but the bird is actually native to which part of the world?
questions.detail: The turkey is native to North America; English speakers mixed it up with the African guinea fowl that reached Europe through Ottoman ('Turkey') trade.
questions.why: The bird's name screams the country Turkey, but Spanish explorers actually brought it back from the Americas.
questions.more: Other languages misfile it too: in Turkish and French the bird's name means 'from India.'
questions.source: Reader's Digest, 'Turkey name origin'; GreekReporter (https://www.rd.com/article/turkey-name-origin/)

questions.cat: Food & Drink · claimed country of origin for a fried snack
questions.dimension: claimed country of origin for a fried snack
questions.q: French fries carry France's name, but a popular claim traces the fried-potato snack to villages along the Meuse River in which neighboring country?
questions.detail: By a widely cited account, villagers in the Meuse valley of Belgium fried potatoes when the river froze and fish ran short, with a recorded mention in Namur by 1680.
questions.why: The 'French' label likely came from American WWI soldiers in French-speaking Belgium, not from France itself.
questions.more: Belgium has even pushed UNESCO to recognize fries as part of its heritage.
questions.source: National Geographic, 'Are French Fries Truly French?' (https://www.nationalgeographic.com/culture/article/are-french-fries-truly-french)

questions.cat: Food & Drink · country where a legume was domesticated
questions.dimension: country where a legume was domesticated
questions.q: The peanut spread worldwide from colonial trade, but genetic and dig evidence place its wild birthplace in which South American country?
questions.detail: The cultivated peanut arose from a natural hybrid of two wild groundnuts in southeastern Bolivia (and nearby Argentina) thousands of years ago.
questions.why: Peanuts feel African or American, but their family tree roots in the Bolivian foothills before any ocean crossing.
questions.more: Peru holds peanut remains dating back over 7,000 years from this early spread.
questions.source: ScienceDirect, 'Origin and Early History of the Peanut'; Plant Humanities Lab (https://phl.juncture-digital.io/peanut)

questions.cat: Food & Drink · country where chilies were domesticated
questions.q: Chili peppers are now central to many Asian cuisines, but scientists trace the domesticated chili's birthplace to which country?
questions.detail: Multiple lines of evidence point to central-east Mexico, from southern Puebla to Veracruz, as where Capsicum annuum was first domesticated over 6,000 years ago.
questions.why: Thai, Indian, and Korean food lean on chilies, yet none had them until Spanish ships carried the Mexican plant across the world.
questions.more: The Tehuacan Valley in Mexico holds chili remains dating back 6,000 to 7,000 years.
questions.source: PNAS, 'Multiple lines of evidence for the origin of domesticated chili pepper' (https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.1308933111)

questions.cat: Food & Drink · native mountain home of a wild crop
questions.dimension: native mountain home of a wild crop
questions.q: Wild tomato plants, the ancestors of every garden tomato, first grew on the slopes of which mountain range?
questions.detail: The small, bitter wild tomato originated in the Andes of western South America, mainly Peru and Ecuador, before being domesticated farther north in Mexico.
questions.why: Italy is the tomato's spiritual home in most minds, but the plant's wild roots are entirely Andean.
questions.more: Tomatoes only reached European kitchens after Spanish contact in the 1500s.
questions.source: Britannica, 'Tomato' (https://www.britannica.com/plant/tomato)

questions.cat: Food & Drink · native country of the coffee plant
questions.dimension: native country of the coffee plant
questions.q: The coffee plant behind most of the world's cups, Coffea arabica, grows wild in the highland forests of which country, its only natural home?
questions.detail: Arabica is native to the southwestern highlands of Ethiopia, the only place wild Arabica still grows in natural forest.
questions.why: Brazil and Colombia dominate coffee output, but the species itself evolved in Ethiopian forests, not the Americas.
questions.more: Arabica makes up roughly three-quarters of global coffee production.
questions.source: Chicago Botanic Garden, 'Coffea arabica'; PMC review of Ethiopian coffee (https://www.chicagobotanic.org/plant-information/plant-finder/coffea-arabica-arabian-coffee)

questions.cat: Food & Drink · true country a 'foreign'-named dessert was cr
questions.dimension: true country a 'foreign'-named dessert was created
questions.q: German chocolate cake, with its coconut-pecan topping, is named not for the country but for a man named German from which country, where the cake was created?
questions.detail: The cake takes its name from Samuel German, who developed a baking chocolate for Baker's in 1852; the cake itself debuted in a 1957 Dallas newspaper recipe.
questions.why: The name reads like a European import, but dropping the apostrophe from 'German's' hid its all-American origin.
questions.more: Sales of German's chocolate jumped 73 percent the year after the recipe ran.
questions.source: Whats Cooking America, 'German Chocolate Cake History' (https://whatscookingamerica.net/history/cakes/germanchocolatecake.htm)

questions.cat: Food & Drink · country that invented a packaged spice blend
questions.dimension: country that invented a packaged spice blend
questions.q: Curry powder, the yellow blend sold in jars, was not an Indian invention. It was first packaged and sold commercially in which country?
questions.detail: Curry powder was created in Britain, first sold commercially in London in 1784, to help colonists mimic Indian flavors back home.
questions.why: Indian kitchens use regional mixes like garam masala, not a single standardized 'curry powder' that the British bottled.
questions.more: The word 'curry' itself comes from the Tamil 'kari,' meaning sauce.
questions.source: Premium Spices, 'History of Curry Powder'; Alibaba spice basics (https://www.premiumspices.co.nz/blogs/news/history-of-curry-powder)

questions.cat: Food & Drink · country holding the wild ancestor of a fruit
questions.dimension: country holding the wild ancestor of a fruit
questions.q: Every cultivated apple traces back to a wild ancestor still growing in the mountain forests of which Central Asian country?
questions.detail: The wild apple Malus sieversii grows in the Tian Shan mountains of Kazakhstan, near Almaty, whose old name Alma-Ata means 'father of apples.'
questions.why: Apples seem European or American, but DNA traces the domestic apple straight back to Kazakh wild forests.
questions.more: Botanist Nikolai Vavilov first traced the apple's genome to a grove near Almaty.
questions.source: Smithsonian Magazine, 'Saving the Apple's Ancient Ancestor in Kazakhstan' (https://www.smithsonianmag.com/travel/saving-the-apples-ancient-ancestor-in-the-forests-of-kazakhstan-180983493/)

questions.cat: Food & Drink · country where guinea pig is traditional food
questions.dimension: country where guinea pig is traditional food
questions.q: The roasted dish 'cuy,' a special-occasion meal, is the guinea pig, an animal raised for food for over 5,000 years in which Andean country?
questions.detail: Guinea pigs were domesticated in the Andes around 5000 BC and remain a celebrated food in Peru, where 'cuy' is eaten roasted or fried.
questions.why: In most countries the guinea pig is strictly a pet, but in the Andes it has been livestock for millennia.
questions.more: Cuy appears in religious and healing traditions dating to pre-Inca times.
questions.source: Eat Peru, 'Eating Cuy'; Migrationology (https://www.eatperu.com/eating-cuy-guinea-pig-peruvian-delicacy/)

questions.cat: Food & Drink · country where cacao was first used
questions.dimension: country where cacao was first used
questions.q: Chocolate is most associated with Europe and West Africa, but the cacao tree was first used by people in the upper Amazon of which South American country?
questions.detail: The oldest evidence of cacao use, about 5,300 years old, comes from the Mayo-Chinchipe culture in southeast Ecuador's upper Amazon, predating Mesoamerican use.
questions.why: Cacao is tied to the Maya and Aztecs, but its earliest use and greatest wild diversity are in the upper Amazon, far to the south.
questions.more: From there cacao likely traveled north through trade to Central America.
questions.source: National Geographic, 'Chocolate gets its origin story rewritten'; Nature (Zarrillo et al.) (https://www.nationalgeographic.com/culture/article/chocolate-domestication-cocoa-ecuador)

questions.cat: Food & Drink · African country where watermelon was domestic
questions.dimension: African country where watermelon was domesticated
questions.q: A recent genetic study traced the domesticated watermelon back to a wild melon from the Kordofan region of which African country?
questions.detail: DNA work showed the closest wild relative of the sweet watermelon is the Kordofan melon of Sudan in northeast Africa, overturning a South African theory.
questions.why: Watermelon feels like an American summer crop, but its true origin is the dry savanna of northeast Africa.
questions.more: Egyptian tomb paintings suggest watermelon was eaten in the Nile Valley over 4,000 years ago.
questions.source: Smithsonian Magazine, 'Researchers Uncover the Watermelon's Origins' (https://www.smithsonianmag.com/smart-news/researchers-uncover-watermelons-origins-180977872/)

questions.cat: Food & Drink · true biological identity of a misnamed food
questions.dimension: true biological identity of a misnamed food
questions.q: On Indian menus, 'Bombay duck' is a fried delicacy. What kind of creature is it actually?
questions.detail: Bombay duck is the lizardfish Harpadon nehereus, common in the Bay of Bengal; the 'duck' likely came from 'Bombay daak' (Bombay mail) on the railways.
questions.why: The name says poultry, but it is a slim ocean fish, usually dried and fried.
questions.more: It is especially popular in India and Bangladesh.
questions.source: Wikipedia, 'Bombay duck' (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bombay_duck)

questions.cat: Food & Drink · true body part behind a misleading food name
questions.dimension: true body part behind a misleading food name
questions.q: On a fancy menu, 'sweetbreads' are not bread and not sweet. What part of a young animal are they?
questions.detail: Sweetbreads are offal from the thymus (throat) or pancreas (stomach) of a young calf or lamb, prized for their rich, creamy texture.
questions.why: The word 'bread' here comes from an old word for food or meat, not baked bread, and tricks most diners.
questions.more: Because the thymus shrinks with age, sweetbreads come only from young animals, making them a delicacy.
questions.source: Britannica, 'Sweetbread' (https://www.britannica.com/topic/sweetbread)

questions.cat: Food & Drink · true biological kingdom of a misnamed food
questions.dimension: true biological kingdom of a misnamed food
questions.q: Sea cucumber, served in Chinese banquets and stews, looks like a vegetable but is really what?
questions.detail: Sea cucumbers are marine animals, echinoderms in the same group as starfish and sea urchins, not plants of any kind.
questions.why: The cucumber shape and name suggest produce, but it is a bottom-dwelling invertebrate.
questions.more: There are about 1,200 species of sea cucumber worldwide.
questions.source: Britannica, 'Sea cucumber'; National Geographic (https://www.britannica.com/animal/sea-cucumber)

questions.cat: Food & Drink · robusta coffee production
questions.q: Brazil grows the most coffee overall, but for the robusta bean that fills most instant coffee, which country is the top grower?
questions.detail: Vietnam grows over 40% of the world's robusta, the hardy bean used heavily in instant coffee and espresso blends; robusta is about 97% of Vietnam's crop.
questions.why: The reflex answer is Brazil, the overall coffee leader, but Brazil's strength is arabica. Sliced to robusta only, Vietnam tops the list.
questions.more: Brazil is now expanding its own robusta plantings and may challenge Vietnam in coming years.
questions.source: Global Coffee Report / intelligence.coffee, robusta production figures

questions.cat: Food & Drink · coffee consumption per capita
questions.q: Which country drinks the most coffee per person, far more than coffee-growing nations?
questions.detail: Finland consumes roughly 12 kilograms of coffee per person per year, the highest in the world, with the other Nordic countries close behind.
questions.why: People expect a producer like Brazil or a big-population drinker, but the heaviest per-person drinkers are cold northern countries that grow no coffee at all.
questions.more: Finnish workplaces traditionally include paid coffee breaks built into the day.
questions.source: WorldAtlas, Top Coffee-Consuming Countries

questions.cat: Food & Drink · vodka consumption per capita
questions.q: Which country drinks the most vodka per person?
questions.detail: Russia leads vodka drinking per person and produces about 30% of the world's vodka, with more than 90% of it consumed at home.
questions.why: Poland and the Nordic countries are strong vodka cultures, but Russia is still both the top producer and the heaviest per-person drinker.
questions.more: On exports the picture differs, with Sweden and Poland challenging Russia.
questions.source: WorldAtlas / Mashed, vodka production and consumption

questions.cat: Food & Drink · hazelnut production
questions.q: Which country produces the most hazelnuts, the nut blended into many coffee and chocolate drinks, growing about 70% of the world's supply?
questions.detail: Turkey grows around 70% of the world's hazelnuts, concentrated in its Black Sea 'hazelnut belt.'
questions.why: Italy is linked with hazelnut flavor through famous spreads, but the nuts themselves come overwhelmingly from Turkey.
questions.more: Turkey's Ordu, Giresun, and Trabzon provinces dominate the crop.
questions.source: Tasting Table; Anadolu Agency, world's largest hazelnut producer

questions.cat: Food & Drink · tea plant species identity
questions.q: Tea leaves for both green and black tea come from the same plant. What is that plant called?
questions.detail: Green, black, white, and oolong teas all come from Camellia sinensis; the difference is how much the picked leaves are oxidized.
questions.why: People assume green and black tea come from different plants, but they are the same species processed differently.
questions.more: Green tea is heated fast to stop oxidation; black tea is fully oxidized.
questions.source: Wikipedia, Camellia sinensis; Smith Teamaker, oxidation in teamaking

questions.cat: Food & Drink · rooibos origin country
questions.q: Rooibos, the caffeine-free red 'bush tea,' grows commercially in only one country. Which one?
questions.detail: Rooibos is native to and grown almost only in South Africa's Cederberg region of the Western Cape.
questions.why: It sells worldwide as a tea, so people guess a big tea nation, but the plant grows commercially in just one small region of South Africa.
questions.more: Rooibos holds a Protected Designation of Origin tied to its South African terroir.
questions.source: Dammann / SouthAfrica.net, rooibos in the Cederberg

questions.cat: Food & Drink · tonic water bittering ingredient
questions.q: What is the bitter ingredient that gives tonic water its sharp taste?
questions.detail: Tonic water is carbonated water with quinine, a bitter compound originally drawn from the bark of the cinchona tree.
questions.why: People know tonic tastes bitter but rarely name quinine, which was first added as a malaria treatment, not a flavoring.
questions.more: Modern tonic has far less quinine than the original medicinal versions.
questions.source: Wikipedia, Tonic water; Medical News Today, quinine in tonic water

questions.cat: Food & Drink · tequila base plant
questions.q: Real tequila must be made from the sugars of which single plant?
questions.detail: By law tequila must be made from the blue Weber agave, with at least 51% of its sugars from that plant; premium bottles use 100% agave.
questions.why: Many think tequila is a cactus spirit, but agave is a succulent, not a cactus, and only the blue Weber variety counts.
questions.more: Tequila can legally be made only in Jalisco and parts of four other Mexican states.
questions.source: Wikipedia, Tequila; Tequila Authority, blue agave

questions.cat: Food & Drink · sake base grain
questions.q: Sake is often called rice wine, but in brewing terms it is made more like beer. What grain is it brewed from?
questions.detail: Sake is brewed from rice, water, koji mold, and yeast, with starch turning to sugar and then alcohol much like in beer.
questions.why: The 'rice wine' label hints at the answer, but the trick is that it is brewed like beer rather than fermented like grape wine.
questions.more: Premium sake usually lands around 15 to 17% alcohol.
questions.source: Wikipedia, Sake; SushiSushi, Japanese rice wine

questions.cat: Food & Drink · cognac origin country
questions.q: Cognac, the famous grape brandy, must be made in which country?
questions.detail: Cognac is a brandy made only in the Charente region of France, distilled twice in copper pot stills and aged in oak.
questions.why: Brandy is made worldwide, but the name Cognac is legally reserved for one French region, so the answer is fixed.
questions.more: Most cognac starts from the Ugni Blanc grape.
questions.source: Britannica, Cognac; Royan Atlantique, Cognac production

questions.cat: Food & Drink · absinthe origin country
questions.q: Absinthe, the green anise spirit nicknamed the 'green fairy,' was first made in which country?
questions.detail: Absinthe was born in the Val-de-Travers valley of Switzerland in the late 1700s before becoming a craze in France.
questions.why: Absinthe is so tied to Parisian cafe culture that France seems obvious, but the drink was invented across the border in Switzerland.
questions.more: Pernod's famous distillery later moved production just into France at Pontarlier.
questions.source: SWI swissinfo.ch; Wormwood Society, history of absinthe

questions.cat: Food & Drink · guinness origin country
questions.q: Guinness stout was first brewed in which country?
questions.detail: Arthur Guinness founded his brewery at St. James's Gate in Dublin, Ireland, in 1759, signing a 9,000-year lease.
questions.why: Guinness is sold worldwide and brewed in dozens of countries, but its origin is firmly Dublin, Ireland.
questions.more: Guinness shifted from ale to dark porter in 1778 and dropped ale entirely by 1799.
questions.source: Britannica, Guinness; Guinness Storehouse, story of Guinness

questions.cat: Food & Drink · bourbon production by state
questions.q: Which U.S. state's distillers produce the large majority of the world's bourbon, by industry count about 95%?
questions.detail: The Kentucky Distillers' Association says about 95% of the world's bourbon is made in Kentucky, though some experts argue the true share is lower.
questions.why: Bourbon is an American whiskey that can legally be made in any state, so people assume it is spread out, but production is heavily concentrated in Kentucky.
questions.more: Counting Tennessee whiskey and craft distillers elsewhere, some estimates put Kentucky nearer 75%.
questions.source: InsideHook; VinePair, how much bourbon is made in Kentucky

questions.cat: Food & Drink · sherry origin country
questions.q: Sherry, the fortified wine from the Jerez area, comes from which country?
questions.detail: Sherry is made around Jerez in southern Spain, fortified after fermentation finishes, which is why it tends to be drier than port.
questions.why: It pairs in people's minds with Portuguese port, but sherry is Spanish, defined by the Jerez region.
questions.more: Sherry's main grape is Palomino, and a yeast layer called flor shapes many styles.
questions.source: Lustau; Diffen, Port vs Sherry

questions.cat: Food & Drink · champagne main grape count
questions.q: Champagne uses three main grape varieties. How many is that?
questions.detail: The three main Champagne grapes are Chardonnay, Pinot Noir, and Pinot Meunier, together about 99% of the region's vines.
questions.why: People picture Champagne as a single white wine, so they overlook that two of its three core grapes are actually red.
questions.more: Five rarer grapes are also permitted but make up under 1% of plantings.
questions.source: Champagne.fr, grape varieties; Wine Enthusiast, Champagne grapes guide

questions.cat: Food & Drink · condiment birthplace country
questions.q: Worcestershire sauce was first bottled and sold by two chemists in a single English city. The sauce now carries that city's name. In which country was it created?
questions.detail: Lea & Perrins began selling their Worcestershire sauce in 1837 from the city of Worcester, England; the recipe was inspired by a fermented fish-and-tamarind sauce from Bengal.
questions.why: The reflex is to chase the 'exotic' Bengali roots, but the commercial sauce that bears the name was invented and bottled in England.
questions.more: It is essentially a fermented fish sauce: anchovies, tamarind, molasses, onion, garlic and vinegar.
questions.source: Britannica; Wikipedia, 'Worcestershire sauce'

questions.cat: Food & Drink · pastry true origin country
questions.q: Croissants are the symbol of the French bakery, but the crescent roll they grew from, the kipferl, came from another country's bakeries first. Which country?
questions.detail: The croissant descends from the Austrian kipferl; Viennese baker August Zang opened a Paris shop in 1839 that inspired French bakers to develop the laminated version.
questions.why: France is the obvious guess, but the crescent shape and the ancestor roll are Austrian; the French innovation was the puff-pastry lamination, not the shape.
questions.more: The 1683 Siege of Vienna victory legend is unsupported; it first appears in print 250 years later.
questions.source: Institute of Culinary Education; Europeana

questions.cat: Food & Drink · spread invention precedence (brand)
questions.q: Vegemite was created to copy an older British yeast-extract spread made from leftover brewer's yeast. Which spread came first?
questions.detail: Marmite was commercialized in Britain in 1902; Vegemite was made in Melbourne in 1923 specifically to imitate it after British imports were disrupted.
questions.why: Australians treat Vegemite as the original icon, but it was the copy; Marmite predates it by two decades.
questions.more: Both rest on brewer's yeast left over from beer brewing.
questions.source: Britannica; National Museum of Australia

questions.cat: Food & Drink · spice top producer (by spice)
questions.q: Indonesia, not Sri Lanka or China, leads the world in producing one common baking spice, mostly the cassia type grown on a Sumatran volcano. Which spice?
questions.detail: Indonesia is the world's largest cinnamon producer, supplying over 40% of global output, mostly cassia (Cinnamomum burmannii) from the slopes of Mount Kerinci in Sumatra.
questions.why: People link cinnamon to Sri Lanka (Ceylon), but the bulk of the world's cinnamon is Indonesian cassia, a different species.
questions.more: Ceylon ('true') cinnamon is a smaller, milder market than cassia.
questions.source: WorldAtlas; 88 Acres / industry data

questions.cat: Food & Drink · spice top exporter country
questions.q: Guatemala, not India, is the world's largest producer of one prized green spice pod, exporting most of the global supply. Which spice?
questions.detail: Guatemala produces and exports the bulk of the world's cardamom, around 80% of exports, despite cardamom being native to and famous from southern India.
questions.why: Cardamom feels quintessentially Indian, but the trade is dominated by Guatemala, where it was introduced in the 20th century.
questions.more: It is sometimes called Guatemala's 'green gold.'
questions.source: USDA FAS report; Sunday Guardian

questions.cat: Food & Drink · nut crop world-share concentration
questions.q: One nut crop is grown almost entirely in a single U.S. state, which supplies roughly three-quarters of the world's harvest. Which nut?
questions.detail: California grows about 75-80% of the world's almonds; the U.S. accounts for roughly 78% of global production, nearly all of it from California's Central Valley.
questions.why: Many guess Mediterranean origins, but one Californian valley dominates the world's almond supply today.
questions.more: Australia and Spain are distant runners-up.
questions.source: Agricultural Marketing Resource Center; industry data

questions.cat: Food & Drink · mango top producer country
questions.q: One country grows nearly half the world's mangoes, far more than any rival, with a single one of its states out-producing most nations. Which country?
questions.detail: India produces roughly 45% of the world's mangoes, about 25 million tonnes a year, with Uttar Pradesh the leading state.
questions.why: Mango feels pan-tropical, but India's output dwarfs all other producers combined enough to claim near half the world.
questions.more: It is the runaway leader despite exporting a relatively small share.
questions.source: World Population Review; Adda247

questions.cat: Food & Drink · soft drink invention decade
questions.q: Coca-Cola was first mixed and sold by an Atlanta pharmacist as a fountain drink. In which decade was it created?
questions.detail: John Pemberton created Coca-Cola in May 1886 in Atlanta, Georgia, first sold at Jacobs' Pharmacy for five cents a glass.
questions.why: Coke feels like a 20th-century icon, but it predates the automobile, born in the 1880s.
questions.more: Bookkeeper Frank Robinson named it and designed the script logo.
questions.source: Coca-Cola Company history; Library of Congress

questions.cat: Food & Drink · fast food chain founding decade
questions.q: The McDonald brothers opened their first restaurant in San Bernardino, California, years before their famous Speedee assembly line. In which decade did the first one open?
questions.detail: Richard and Maurice McDonald opened their first restaurant in 1940 in San Bernardino; the Speedee Service System came in 1948, before Ray Kroc expanded the chain.
questions.why: People date McDonald's to Kroc's 1950s expansion, but the original restaurant opened a decade earlier.
questions.more: The first menu centered on barbecue served by carhops.
questions.source: History of McDonald's, Wikipedia; PBS SoCal

questions.cat: Food & Drink · dessert invention decade
questions.q: The chocolate chip cookie was created by accident at a New England inn when a cook chopped a chocolate bar into the dough. In which decade?
questions.detail: Ruth Wakefield invented the cookie in 1938 at the Toll House Inn in Whitman, Massachusetts, and the recipe appeared in her 1938 cookbook.
questions.why: It feels timeless, but it is younger than many think and was born by improvisation, not design.
questions.more: Wakefield sold the rights and Toll House name to Nestle in 1939.
questions.source: Sugar.org; Lemelson-MIT

questions.cat: Food & Drink · snack invention decade (motive)
questions.q: Graham crackers were invented by a clergyman who pushed a bland diet to curb appetite and desire, long before they topped any campfire treat. In which decade were they devised?
questions.detail: Sylvester Graham devised the graham cracker around 1829 as part of a bland vegetarian regimen meant to suppress carnal urges; the original was coarse, unsweetened graham flour.
questions.why: Sweet s'mores crackers feel modern, but they began as a near-medicinal health food in the 1820s.
questions.more: Today's sweet version bears little resemblance to Graham's original.
questions.source: All That's Interesting; JSTOR Daily

questions.cat: Food & Drink · preservation method invention decade
questions.q: Modern canning was invented to win a French government prize for feeding armies, decades before bacteria were understood. In which decade was the winning method recognized?
questions.detail: Nicolas Appert won the 12,000-franc French prize in 1809-1810 for sealing food in glass jars and boiling them; he published the first modern preservation book in 1810.
questions.why: Canning seems industrial-era, but it was solved by a confectioner in the Napoleonic 1800s, before germ theory.
questions.more: Appert is called the father of canning; the tin can came shortly after.
questions.source: Encyclopedia.com; Nesta

questions.cat: Food & Drink · spice botany (two spices, one fruit)
questions.q: Nutmeg and mace are sold as two separate spices, but they are harvested from a single fruit of one tree. Mace is which part of that fruit?
questions.detail: Both come from Myristica fragrans: nutmeg is the seed, and mace is the lacy red aril wrapping the seed; it is the only plant yielding two distinct spices from one fruit.
questions.why: They taste and look different, so people assume two plants; in fact mace is just the seed's outer net.
questions.more: The fruit's flesh is sometimes candied separately.
questions.source: Wikipedia, 'Nutmeg'; The Spice House

questions.cat: Food & Drink · spirit legal definition (grain threshold)
questions.q: To be legally called bourbon, the grain mash must be made mostly from one grain, with a minimum majority share set by U.S. rules. Which grain, and what minimum share?
questions.detail: U.S. standards require bourbon mash to be at least 51% corn, distilled and aged in new charred oak in the United States; the 51% rule dates to the 1935 Standards of Identity.
questions.why: Many know bourbon is corn-based but not the exact legal floor; rye or wheat fill the rest.
questions.more: It must also be aged in new charred oak and made in the U.S.
questions.source: Long Road Distillers; LegalClarity

questions.cat: Food & Drink · beverage defining method (appellation)
questions.q: Champagne can only be labeled 'Champagne' if it comes from one specific region and uses a defining production step. What is that defining method?
questions.answer_subject: secondary fermentation in the bottle (traditional method)
questions.state: secondary fermentation in the bottle (traditional method)
questions.detail: True Champagne must come from the Champagne AOC in France and undergo a second fermentation inside the bottle, the methode champenoise, protected by law since the 1800s.
questions.why: People think 'Champagne' just means bubbly wine, but the bubbles must form via a bottle re-fermentation in that one region.
questions.more: Pinot noir, Pinot meunier and Chardonnay are the main grapes.
questions.source: Union des Maisons de Champagne; MasterClass

questions.cat: Food & Drink · produce structure count (achenes)
questions.q: The familiar 'seeds' speckling a strawberry's surface are actually tiny separate fruits. About how many sit on one average garden strawberry, to the nearest hundred?
questions.detail: A typical garden strawberry carries roughly 200 achenes on its surface (commonly cited range 150-400); each achene is a dry fruit holding one seed.
questions.why: People picture a handful of seeds, but a single berry is studded with on the order of two hundred.
questions.more: Each speck is a fruit; the red flesh is a swollen flower base.
questions.source: Biology Insights; Chef's Resource

questions.cat: Food & Drink · cultivar count (apples)
questions.q: Despite a few dozen apples filling supermarket bins, the number of named apple varieties grown worldwide runs into the thousands. The most-cited figure is over how many?
questions.detail: More than 7,500 apple cultivars are documented worldwide, yet only about 25 are grown commercially; some estimates run far higher.
questions.why: Shoppers see maybe a dozen kinds, masking a domesticated diversity in the thousands.
questions.more: The wild ancestor traces to the Caucasus and Central Asia.
questions.source: Wikipedia, 'List of apple cultivars'; Grow Organic
